intended_use: IB Lab LAMA is a fully-automated radiological image processing software device intended to aid users in the measurement of limb-length discrepancy and quantitative knee alignment parameters on uni- an
intended_use_full: IB Lab LAMA is a fully-automated radiological image processing software device intended to aid users in the measurement of limb-length discrepancy and quantitative knee alignment parameters on uni- and bilateral AP full leg radiographs of individuals at least 22 years of age. It should not be used in-lieu of full patient evaluation or solely relied upon to make or confirm a diagnosis. The software device is intended to be used by healthcare professionals trained in radiology. IB Lab LAMA is not indicated for use on radiographs on which Ankle Arthroplasties and/or Unicompartmental Knee Arthroplasties are present.
target_condition: suspected or present deformities of the lower extremities; limb-length discrepancy; knee alignment abnormalities
ground_truth: Independent measurements by two US Board-certified musculoskeletal radiologists with at least 5 years post-fellowship expertise, blinded to clinical reports and each other's assessments; measurements averaged and consensus read performed when differences exceeded threshold
other_metrics: Bland-Altman plots and intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) computed for agreement; mechanical axis deviation mean difference -1.37 mm (SD 2.54), femur length mean difference 0.09 cm (SD 0.16), tibia length mean difference 0.01 cm (SD 0.13), leg length discrepancy mean difference 0.13 mm (SD 1.4), hip knee ankle angle mean difference -0.19° (SD 0.73), anatomical tibiofemoral angle mean difference -0.51° (SD 1.18), anatomical mechanical angle mean difference 0.06° (SD 0.89), joint-line convergence angle mean difference 0.22° (SD 1.35), mechanical lateral proximal femoral angle mean difference 2.48° (SD 2.45), mechanical lateral distal femoral angle mean difference -0.37° (SD 0.96), mechanical medial proximal tibia angle mean difference -0.01° (SD 1.28), mechanical lateral distal tibia angle mean difference -0.62° (SD 2.05); failure rate ~2.8% (9 of 324 legs); repeatability testing showed no differences between repeated runs
